summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing a severe energy affordability crisis, with statewide average residential electric bills spiking 17.5% in a single year (2024–2025) — the largest year-over-year jump since at least 2005 — driven by a wave of large rate increases across all five major investor-owned utilities. In response, Gov. Mike Braun signed landmark utility reform legislation (HEA 1002) in April 2026 introducing performance-based ratemaking, and the IURC launched an unprecedented Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability in March 2026, publicly questioning all five major utilities about rising bills. Low-income Hoosiers face particular pressure as LIHEAP faces ongoing federal threats to its funding, even as Indiana's winter disconnection moratorium and new summer shut-off protections under HEA 1002 provide some protection.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households on fixed incomes
messaging_recommendations.message: You have legal protections — Indiana's winter disconnection moratorium shields LIHEAP-eligible customers from shutoff Dec. 1 through March 15, and new 2026 law adds summer heat-index protections; contact your local Community Action Agency to apply for energy assistance before the fall 2026 application window opens.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Indiana state legislators and IURC commissioners
messaging_recommendations.message: Performance-based ratemaking under HEA 1002 is a critical first step, but robust enforcement and mandatory low-income affordability programs are needed now — Indiana's five major utilities collectively raised residential bills an average of 17.5% in one year, and roughly one in five Hoosiers are not covered by current regulatory protections.

Indiana's electricity bills have risen faster than almost any state in the country in recent years — NIPSCO customers are paying 90% more than in 2016, and CenterPoint customers average $221/month — framing these increases as a systemic regulatory failure, not an inevitable market outcome, builds public support for structural reform.

Transparency and accountability are now legal requirements: HEA 1002 mandates Multi-Year Rate Plans with customer affordability metrics tied to CPI, and utilities that fail to meet performance benchmarks risk reduced returns on equity — advocates should monitor and publicize compliance closely.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana sought a base rate increase of approximately $491.5 million (originally proposed as ~16% for residential customers) to cover infrastructure, coal ash compliance, and grid investments. The IURC approved an increase of approximately $295.7 million in annual revenues, phased in across 2025 and 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approx. $19.16/month increase for average residential customer; ~$295.7M annual revenue increase approved (vs. $491.5M requested)

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana sought a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity (CPCN) to retire two coal units at the Cayuga Generating Station and replace them with two new combined-cycle natural gas units (~738 MW each), with project costs estimated at approximately $3.3 billion including financing. The IURC approved the plan in October 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: Costs to be recovered through future rate increases; project estimated at ~$3.33B including AFUDC and reserves

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy sought recovery of approximately $88 million in coal ash cleanup costs through customer rates. The Indiana Court of Appeals ruled in August 2025 that the IURC 'impermissibly applied' Indiana law retroactively when it approved this rate hike, potentially requiring Duke to revise its rate recovery.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$88M in coal ash cost recovery at issue; ruling may require refund or rate adjustment

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed a $192.9 million base rate increase request in June 2025, which would have raised typical residential bills approximately $21/month in two phases (7.5% in Q2 2026 and ~6% in January 2027). A partial settlement was reached in October 2025 for approximately $90 million, but was opposed by both the OUCC and the Citizens Action Coalition; the case is fully briefed with a final IURC order deadline of June 24, 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Originally $192.9M requested; settlement proposes ~$90M increase; OUCC recommends $21.2M rate reduction from current rates

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO sought a base electric rate increase of approximately $368.7 million, citing over $2 billion in capital investments for its energy transition. A settlement agreement reduced the approved increase to approximately $257 million, phased in from July 2025 through early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approx. $23/month (16.75%) for average residential customer using 672 kWh/month; phased in July 2025–Q1 2026

rate_cases.description: IURC opened a formal investigation in November 2025 into NIPSCO's natural gas billing discrepancies stemming from faulty meter indexes during the rollout of Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI). Unsealed records confirmed approximately 3,542 customers were double-billed; an evidentiary hearing is scheduled for July 2, 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Refunds being issued to approximately 3,542 overcharged customers; amount TBD

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy Indiana's electric rate case, filed in December 2023, was approved by the IURC in February 2025 for an $80 million annual revenue increase (vs. ~$118.8 million originally requested), implemented in multiple phases. The final phase of approximately $6.86/month for average residential customers took effect in March 2026, after which CenterPoint committed to stable rates through 2027.
rate_cases.rate_change: $80M annual revenue increase approved; final phase of ~$6.86/month effective March 2026; total increase approx. 17.4% over all phases

rate_cases.description: Indiana Michigan Power received IURC approval in May 2024 for a settlement of $61.8 million in annual rate increases (base rates of $56.9M plus $4.9M in rider revenues), phased in over two steps. I&M's president Steve Baker noted at the March 2026 IURC affordability hearing that the company plans to seek a rate decrease in a future proceeding.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$61.8M total annual revenue increase; average residential bills approx. $167/month as of July 2025 (4% increase year-over-year)

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: The IURC held a landmark five-hour Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability on March 24, 2026, publicly questioning the leaders of Indiana's five largest investor-owned electric utilities about rising bills. According to the IURC's own survey data, NIPSCO customers averaged $234/month (up 27%) and CenterPoint customers averaged $221/month (up 25%) as of July 2025. Utility executives largely attributed high bills to customer usage and infrastructure investment costs, while regulators and lawmakers expressed frustration with the lack of concrete solutions offered.

articles.headline: Braun signs HEA 1002 to lower Indiana energy costs
articles.summary: Gov. Mike Braun signed House Enrolled Act 1002 into law on April 7, 2026, overhauling Indiana's utility regulatory framework by introducing performance-based ratemaking that requires utilities to meet measurable benchmarks for affordability and reliability. Under the new law, returns on equity are no longer guaranteed, and utilities must demonstrate accountability to customers. The law also mandates low-income customer assistance programs, predictable billing plans, and a summer disconnection moratorium during extreme heat events.

articles.headline: NIPSCO overcharged 3,500 natural gas customers, newly unsealed records show
articles.summary: Newly unsealed records revealed NIPSCO admitted to overcharging approximately 3,542 natural gas customers — billing them double their actual usage — due to incorrectly installed mechanical meter indexes that were not rotating at the correct drive rate. NIPSCO did not initially inform state regulators about the full extent of the problem. The company said affected customers would receive refunds for 12 months of overcharges, and a formal IURC evidentiary hearing on the matter is scheduled for July 2, 2026.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: In an unusual move, IURC Chair Andy Zay announced an Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability, calling in Indiana's five largest investor-owned electric utilities — AES Indiana, CenterPoint Energy, Duke Energy, Indiana Michigan Power, and NIPSCO — for a public accountability session. Zay stated the balance between utility costs and affordability 'feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers.' The inquiry is designed to gather information on rate structures, billing transparency, and short-term relief options.

articles.headline: Hoosiers' electric bills spike 17.5% in one year
articles.summary: A Citizens Action Coalition analysis of IURC data found that Indiana's average residential electric bills increased 17.5% year-over-year from July 2024 to July 2025 — the highest jump since at least 2005 and nearly double the prior year's record increase of 9.3%. NIPSCO customers were hit hardest with a $50/month (26.7%) increase, followed by CenterPoint at approximately $44/month. CAC Executive Director Kerwin Olson condemned the trend, saying state leaders were continuing to allow it to happen.

articles.headline: State's ratepayer advocate recommends denial of AES' $200M proposed increase
articles.summary: The Indiana OUCC filed testimony from 11 agency experts recommending the IURC not only deny AES Indiana's $192.9 million rate increase request but actually cut current rates by $21.2 million. The filing included over 6,800 written consumer comments — more than any recent individual utility rate case. The OUCC's recommendation also included reducing AES's monthly customer service charge from $17 to $11.25 rather than AES's proposed increase to $20.

articles.headline: Duke's $3.3B coal-to-gas conversion, third-party coal study approved
articles.summary: Indiana regulators approved Duke Energy Indiana's $3.3 billion plan to replace two coal-fired units at the Cayuga Generating Station with natural gas combined-cycle units, a project that will be funded through gradual future rate increases to customers. Duke also filed a separate compliance filing in November 2025 related to its earlier base rate order, indicating further rate adjustment proceedings. Consumer advocates raised concerns that the project locks ratepayers into decades of natural gas costs.

articles.headline: Indiana representative calling for review of NIPSCO rates
articles.summary: State Rep. Jim Pressel (R-Rolling Prairie) sent a letter to IURC Chair Andy Zay calling for a review of NIPSCO's escalating rates after constituents in northern Indiana reported dramatic bill increases. According to the IURC's 2025 Electricity Residential Bill Survey, NIPSCO customers saw bills for 1,000 kWh in July rise over 90% compared to 2016. Pressel asked the IURC to evaluate NIPSCO's investments over the past decade, improve billing transparency, and find short-term rate stabilization measures.

articles.headline: Indiana's Energy Assistance Program now open: What you need to know
articles.summary: Indiana's Energy Assistance Program (EAP/LIHEAP) opened for the 2025–2026 application season on October 1, 2025, accepting applications through April 20, 2026. The program, funded by federal LIHEAP dollars, provides a one-time annual benefit toward heating and electric bills for eligible low-income households. Indiana received $87.1 million in FY2026 LIHEAP federal funding, though the program faces potential elimination in future federal budget cycles under the Trump administration's FY2027 proposals.

articles.headline: Evansville City Council calls for pause on CenterPoint Energy rate increases
articles.summary: The Evansville City Council passed a resolution 6-0 urging the IURC to pause all electricity rate hikes by CenterPoint Energy in the city. The action came less than a week after the IURC held an energy affordability listening session in Evansville as part of its statewide inquiry. CenterPoint stated it had committed to keeping base rates stable through 2027, but council members and residents pointed to ongoing tracker-based charges continuing to increase bills beyond base rates.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: "That balance feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers and today is the beginning of a process that we are labeling an investigative inquiry. … We're asking our five investor-owned utilities that we regulate on a week-in, week-out basis … to come to this room and have an open dialog about affordability."
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / IURC press conference

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: "In my short time as chairman of the IURC, my fellow commissioners and I have heard from thousands of utility customers from all over Indiana that they are struggling to pay their energy bills."
stakeholders.source: IURC news release / IURC listening sessions announcement

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: "I'm not dismissing, I'm not … deflecting responsibility. We have a role to play in this, absolutely … but that's a culture we're working on."
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Journal Gazette — Big Five utilities affordability hearing coverage

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving Commission approval in its last rate case."

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "The settlement agreement filed by AES and other parties shows the utility's initial request was inflated and unnecessary. The OUCC stands by its initial recommendations for a reduction from current rates and will oppose the utility's settlement agreement."
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release / WTHR / Daily Journal

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "We received thousands of consumer comments in this case, highlighting serious hardships many customers are facing, and I want to thank the individuals who took time to submit comments either in writing or at the IURC's public hearings."
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release on AES Indiana rate case (Cause No. 46258)

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "Things keeps getting worse for Hoosier consumers and our state leaders continue to allow it to happen. Based on this trend, the rhetoric regarding affordability emanating from the Statehouse appears to be nothing more than lip service."
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition press release / Indiana Capital Chronicle

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "For too long, Hoosiers have been footing the bill for decisions they didn't make and policies they didn't ask for. Indiana's regulatory system should be built to protect consumers, but that goal can only be achieved if lawmakers give regulators the tools and authority it needs. The solutions have been ready for years. The need is now incredibly urgent."
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition / WBIW — ICV and CAC policy plan announcement

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "We will vigorously oppose this unfair and unaffordable deal and do everything we can to protect the residents of Indianapolis from yet another large utility bill increase. Hoosiers have had enough with AES Indiana's poor service, soaring bills and backroom deals."
stakeholders.source: CAC press release / Journal Gazette — AES Indiana settlement coverage

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: "Hoosiers have been burdened with utility rate increase after increase. We can't take it anymore. I am directing [the OUCC] to evaluate utilities' profits and find cost-saving measures to ease the financial burden on residents."
stakeholders.source: Governor's press release / ABC57 / Utility Dive

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: "Affordability is this administration's top priority, and high electricity prices have placed a major burden on Hoosiers' wallets. I've taken action to put affordability for Hoosiers above profits for utilities, and this new law requiring utilities to meet performance benchmarks based on affordability and accountability is a big step forward for affordability."
stakeholders.source: Governor's press release — HEA 1002 signing / Brown County Democrat

stakeholders.name: Jim Pressel
stakeholders.title: State Representative (R-Rolling Prairie)
stakeholders.quote: "I was a little disappointed frankly with the responses from NIPSCO. Didn't seem like we were getting answers to the questions that the commissioners were asking. Not sure if it was just the presentation, how that was, but I was really disappointed that we didn't hear something on how can we lower rates."
stakeholders.source: WNDU — Indiana regulators question utility companies on rising electric bills

stakeholders.name: Jim Pressel
stakeholders.title: State Representative (R-Rolling Prairie)
stakeholders.quote: "Hoosiers in our community are struggling with high utility bills and we want to see solutions. We are now asking the IURC and NIPSCO to review current utility rates and take more consideration of the customers."
stakeholders.source: WNDU — Michiana representative calls for NIPSCO rate review

stakeholders.name: Mike Bohacek
stakeholders.title: State Senator (R-Michiana Shores)
stakeholders.quote: "In testimony submitted to the IURC on March 6, NIPSCO testified that Hoosiers were charged for more gas than they consumed due to meter indexes not functioning properly. Utility rates have been increasing around the state, especially in northern Indiana, and having faulty information resulting in incorrect billing is unacceptable."
stakeholders.source: WNDU / The Indiana Lawyer — Sen. Bohacek letter to AG Rokita

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: "Affordability is important, but it can't be focused on at the expense of the other pillars." (Paraphrased: Davis-Handy also acknowledged increased customer frustration, including unspecified threats that led AES to cancel scheduled open houses.)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Public Media / IPM — State commission presses power companies on rates

stakeholders.name: Ben Smaltz
stakeholders.title: State Representative (R-Auburn)
stakeholders.quote: "Hoosiers deserve transparency and accountability when it comes to their energy bills."
stakeholders.source: WFFT — State investigation starts after lawmakers call for probe into NIPSCO rate hikes
